$\dfrac{1}{2}mv_0^2 + mgh = \dfrac{1}{2}mv_1^2$
$\Rightarrow v_1^2 = v_0^2 + 2gh$

During the perfectly elastic collision between block A (mass $m$) and block B (mass $2m$, initially at rest), the velocity $v_B$ of block B just after the collision is:
$v_B = \dfrac{2m}{m + 2m}v_1 = \dfrac{2}{3}v_1$

For block B to reach the sleeping man at height $h$, its kinetic energy just after the collision must be greater than its potential energy at height $h$:
$\dfrac{1}{2}(2m)v_B^2 > (2m)gh$
$\Rightarrow v_B^2 > 2gh$

Substituting $v_B = \dfrac{2}{3}v_1$:
$\left(\dfrac{2}{3}v_1\right)^2 > 2gh$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{4}{9}v_1^2 > 2gh$
$\Rightarrow v_1^2 > \dfrac{18}{4}gh = 4.5gh$

Now, substituting $v_1^2 = v_0^2 + 2gh$:
$v_0^2 + 2gh > 4.5gh$
$\Rightarrow v_0^2 > 2.5gh$
$\Rightarrow v_0 > \sqrt{2.5gh}$

Thus, the initial velocity imparted to block A must be greater than $\sqrt{2.5gh}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A bullet of mass $10 \text{ g}$ travelling horizontally with a speed of $50\sqrt{7} \text{ m/s}$ strikes a $490 \text{ g}$ block placed on a frictionless track, as shown in the figure. The bullet becomes embedded in the block, and the combined system then travels towards a semicircular track of radius $0.2 \text{ m}$. At what location will the block hit the horizontal part after leaving the semicircular track?
options: Exactly at the junction of the straight and curved parts
At a distance of $0.4 \text{ m}$ from the junction on the straight part
At a distance of $0.1\sqrt{3} \text{ m}$ from the junction on the straight part
At a distance of $0.2 \text{ m}$ from the junction on the straight part
questions.solution:
By conservation of linear momentum during the collision:
$m u = (m + M) v$
$0.01 \times 50\sqrt{7} = (0.01 + 0.49) v$
$0.5\sqrt{7} = 0.5 v \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{7} \text{ m/s}$

Let the block leave the semicircular track at an angle $\theta$ above the horizontal diameter. At this point, the normal reaction $N = 0$. The component of weight along the radius provides the necessary centripetal force:
$(m + M)g \sin\theta = \dfrac{(m + M)v'^2}{R}$
$v'^2 = gR \sin\theta = 10 \times 0.2 \times \sin\theta = 2\sin\theta$

By conservation of mechanical energy from the bottom of the track to this point:
$\dfrac{1}{2}(m + M)v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}(m + M)v'^2 + (m + M)gR(1 + \sin\theta)$
$v^2 = v'^2 + 2gR(1 + \sin\theta)$
$7 = 2\sin\theta + 2(10)(0.2)(1 + \sin\theta)$
$7 = 2\sin\theta + 4(1 + \sin\theta)$
$7 = 4 + 6\sin\theta \Rightarrow 6\sin\theta = 3 \Rightarrow \sin\theta = \dfrac{1}{2}$
$\theta = 30^\circ$

The velocity of the block when it leaves the track is:
$v' = \sqrt{2 \sin 30^\circ} = 1 \text{ m/s}$

Taking the junction of the straight and curved parts as the origin $(0,0)$, the position of the block when it leaves the track is:
$x_0 = -R \cos 30^\circ = -0.2 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = -0.1\sqrt{3} \text{ m}$
$y_0 = R + R \sin 30^\circ = 0.2 + 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.3 \text{ m}$

The velocity vector is tangent to the path, making an angle of $90^\circ - \theta = 60^\circ$ with the horizontal.
$v_x = v' \cos 60^\circ = 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$
$v_y = v' \sin 60^\circ = 1 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \text{ m/s}$

For the subsequent projectile motion, the time $t$ to hit the horizontal part $(y = 0)$ is given by:
$y = y_0 + v_y t - \dfrac{1}{2}gt^2$
$0 = 0.3 + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} t - 5t^2$
$50t^2 - 5\sqrt{3}t - 3 = 0$

Solving for $t$ using the quadratic formula:
$t = \dfrac{5\sqrt{3} \pm \sqrt{75 - 4(50)(-3)}}{100} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{3} \pm \sqrt{675}}{100} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{3} \pm 15\sqrt{3}}{100}$
Taking the positive root, $t = \dfrac{20\sqrt{3}}{100} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{5} \text{ s}$

The horizontal position at this time is:
$x = x_0 + v_x t = -0.1\sqrt{3} + 0.5 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{5} = -0.1\sqrt{3} + 0.1\sqrt{3} = 0 \text{ m}$

Thus, the block hits exactly at the junction of the straight and curved parts.

Answer: Exactly at the junction of the straight and curved parts

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: Two balls possessing masses $m$ and $2m$ are tied to two light strings of equal length $l$, as shown in the figure. The other ends of the strings are anchored at a common point $O$. Both strings are held in the same horizontal line before the system is released from rest. Given that the collision between the balls is elastic, determine the velocities of the balls just after the collision.
options: Light ball $\sqrt{\dfrac{50gl}{9}}$ towards left, heavy ball $\sqrt{\dfrac{2gl}{9}}$ towards right
Light ball $\sqrt{\dfrac{25gl}{9}}$ towards left, heavy ball $\sqrt{\dfrac{4gl}{9}}$ towards right
Light ball $\sqrt{\dfrac{50gl}{3}}$ towards left, heavy ball $\sqrt{\dfrac{2gl}{3}}$ towards right
Light ball $\sqrt{\dfrac{2gl}{9}}$ towards left, heavy ball $\sqrt{\dfrac{50gl}{9}}$ towards right
questions.solution:
Let the speed of each ball just before the collision be $v_0$. Since both balls are released from the horizontal position and fall through a height $l$, their speeds are given by:

$v_0 = \sqrt{2gl}$

Let the right direction be positive. The initial velocity of the light ball (mass $m$) is $u_1 = v_0$ and the initial velocity of the heavy ball (mass $2m$) is $u_2 = -v_0$.

Let $v_1$ and $v_2$ be the velocities of the light and heavy balls respectively just after the collision.

By the principle of conservation of linear momentum:

$m u_1 + 2m u_2 = m v_1 + 2m v_2$

$m(v_0) + 2m(-v_0) = m v_1 + 2m v_2$

$v_1 + 2v_2 = -v_0$ ... (i)

Since the collision is elastic, the coefficient of restitution is $e = 1$. The relative velocity of separation equals the relative velocity of approach:

$v_2 - v_1 = u_1 - u_2$

$v_2 - v_1 = v_0 - (-v_0) = 2v_0$ ... (ii)

Adding equations (i) and (ii):

$(v_1 + 2v_2) + (v_2 - v_1) = -v_0 + 2v_0$

$3v_2 = v_0 \Rightarrow v_2 = \dfrac{v_0}{3}$

Substituting $v_2$ into equation (ii):

$\dfrac{v_0}{3} - v_1 = 2v_0 \Rightarrow v_1 = \dfrac{v_0}{3} - 2v_0 = -\dfrac{5v_0}{3}$

Substituting $v_0 = \sqrt{2gl}$:

$v_1 = -\dfrac{5}{3}\sqrt{2gl} = -\sqrt{\dfrac{50gl}{9}}$

$v_2 = \dfrac{1}{3}\sqrt{2gl} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2gl}{9}}$

The negative sign for $v_1$ indicates that the light ball moves towards the left, and the positive sign for $v_2$ indicates that the heavy ball moves towards the right.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: Two friends, Ankit and Bharat, each weighing 40 kg, sit at a separation of d on a frictionless platform. Ankit rolls a 4 kg ball along the platform towards Bharat, who catches it. Bharat subsequently rolls the ball back to Ankit, who catches it. This back-and-forth motion of the ball between the two friends continues. The ball travels at a constant speed of 5 m/s relative to the platform. What is the maximum number of times Ankit can roll the ball?
questions.solution:
Let the mass of Ankit and Bharat be $M = 40$ kg, and the mass of the ball be $m = 4$ kg.
The speed of the ball relative to the platform is constant at $v = 5$ m/s. Thus, the momentum of the ball is always $p = mv = 4 \times 5 = 20$ kg m/s when moving towards Bharat, and $-20$ kg m/s when moving towards Ankit.

Each time Bharat catches the ball, he absorbs $20$ kg m/s of momentum. When he rolls it back, he imparts $-20$ kg m/s to the ball, gaining another $20$ kg m/s. Thus, in each catch-and-throw cycle, Bharat's momentum increases by $40$ kg m/s.

After $(n-1)$ such cycles, Bharat's momentum is $40(n-1)$ kg m/s. Since his mass is $40$ kg, his velocity is:
$v_B = \dfrac{40(n-1)}{40} = n - 1$ m/s.

For Ankit's $n$-th roll to reach Bharat, the velocity of the ball must be strictly greater than Bharat's velocity:
$v_{ball} > v_B \Rightarrow 5 > n - 1 \Rightarrow n < 6$.

This means Ankit's 6th roll will not reach Bharat. However, we must check if Ankit can receive the ball from Bharat's 5th roll to make this 6th roll.

Ankit's momentum after $n$ rolls is $-40n + 20$. For $n = 5$, his momentum is $-40(5) + 20 = -180$ kg m/s, so his velocity is $-180 / 40 = -4.5$ m/s.
Bharat's 5th roll sends the ball towards Ankit at $-5$ m/s. Since the ball's velocity ($-5$ m/s) is more negative than Ankit's velocity ($-4.5$ m/s), the ball will catch up to Ankit.

Ankit catches the ball and rolls it for the 6th time. After this 6th roll, the ball moves at $5$ m/s, but Bharat is also moving at $v_B = 6 - 1 = 5$ m/s. The ball will never reach Bharat, so the back-and-forth motion stops.

Therefore, the maximum number of times Ankit can roll the ball is 6.

Answer: 6

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: Two balls having masses $m$ and $2m$ are affixed to two light strings of identical length $l$. The upper ends of the strings are fixed at point $O$. The system is released from rest while the strings are aligned in the same horizontal line. Following the perfectly elastic collision between the balls at the lowest point, determine the maximum height reached by each ball after the collision.
questions.solution:
Let the velocities of the light ball of mass $m$ and the heavy ball of mass $2m$ just before the collision be $u_1$ and $u_2$ respectively. Since both are released from a horizontal position, they fall through a height $l$.

$u_1 = \sqrt{2gl}$

$u_2 = -\sqrt{2gl}$

For a perfectly elastic collision, the velocities after the collision are given by:

$v_1 = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1 + m_2}u_1 + \dfrac{2m_2}{m_1 + m_2}u_2$

$v_2 = \dfrac{2m_1}{m_1 + m_2}u_1 + \dfrac{m_2 - m_1}{m_1 + m_2}u_2$

Substituting $m_1 = m$ and $m_2 = 2m$:

$v_1 = \dfrac{m - 2m}{3m}(\sqrt{2gl}) + \dfrac{4m}{3m}(-\sqrt{2gl}) = -\dfrac{5}{3}\sqrt{2gl}$

$v_2 = \dfrac{2m}{3m}(\sqrt{2gl}) + \dfrac{2m - m}{3m}(-\sqrt{2gl}) = \dfrac{1}{3}\sqrt{2gl}$

The maximum height reached by the heavy ball is:

$h_2 = \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{9}(2gl)}{2g} = \dfrac{l}{9}$

For the light ball, the theoretical maximum height as a free projectile would be:

$h_1 = \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{\dfrac{25}{9}(2gl)}{2g} = \dfrac{25l}{9}$

Since the light ball is attached to a string of length $l$, it can only reach a maximum height of $2l$ if it completes the vertical circle. The condition to complete the vertical circle is that the velocity at the lowest point must be greater than or equal to $\sqrt{5gl}$.

$|v_1| = \sqrt{\dfrac{50}{9}gl}$

Since $\dfrac{50}{9} > 5$, the light ball completes the vertical circle. Thus, its maximum height is restricted by the string to the top of the circle, which is $2l$.

Answer: Light ball $2l$, heavy ball $l/9$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A ball drops to the ground from a height of $2.0 \text{ m}$ and then rebounds to a height of $1.5 \text{ m}$. Determine the coefficient of restitution.
questions.solution:
The velocity of the ball just before hitting the ground is $v_1 = \sqrt{2gh_1}$

The velocity of the ball just after rebounding from the ground is $v_2 = \sqrt{2gh_2}$

The coefficient of restitution $e$ is given by the ratio of velocity of separation to the velocity of approach:

$e = \dfrac{v_2}{v_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{h_2}{h_1}}$

Given $h_1 = 2.0 \text{ m}$ and $h_2 = 1.5 \text{ m}$

$e = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5}{2.0}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{4}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A uniform chain of mass $M$ and length $L$ is suspended vertically such that its lower end just touches a horizontal floor. The chain is then released from rest. Any portion of the chain that strikes the floor immediately comes to rest. Assuming the chain does not pile up into a heap on the floor, determine the force it exerts on the floor when a length $x$ has reached the floor.
questions.solution:
Let $\lambda = \dfrac{M}{L}$ be the linear mass density of the chain.

When a length $x$ of the chain has reached the floor, the chain has fallen through a distance $x$. The velocity of the falling portion of the chain is $v = \sqrt{2gx}$.

The force exerted by the chain on the floor consists of two components.

The weight of the portion of the chain lying on the floor is given by:
$W = (\lambda x)g = \dfrac{Mgx}{L}$

The thrust force due to the continuous impact of the falling chain is equal to the rate of change of momentum. The rate at which mass strikes the floor is $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \lambda v$. Since the chain comes to rest immediately upon striking the floor, the thrust force is:
$F_{thrust} = v \dfrac{dm}{dt} = v(\lambda v) = \lambda v^2$

Substituting $v^2 = 2gx$, we get:
$F_{thrust} = \lambda (2gx) = \dfrac{2Mgx}{L}$

The total force exerted on the floor is the sum of these two forces:
$F_{total} = W + F_{thrust} = \dfrac{Mgx}{L} + \dfrac{2Mgx}{L} = \dfrac{3Mgx}{L}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a block of mass $2.0 \text{ kg}$ travels at a velocity of $1.0 \text{ m/s}$ on a frictionless horizontal surface towards a second block of equal mass that is at rest. The spring attached to the stationary block has a spring constant of $100 \text{ N/m}$. Determine the maximum compression of the spring.
questions.solution:
Let $m_1 = 2.0 \text{ kg}$, $u_1 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$, $m_2 = 2.0 \text{ kg}$, $u_2 = 0 \text{ m/s}$, and $k = 100 \text{ N/m}$.

At the instant of maximum compression, both blocks move with the same common velocity $v$.

By conservation of linear momentum:

$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = (m_1 + m_2) v$

$2.0 \times 1.0 + 0 = (2.0 + 2.0) v$

$2.0 = 4.0 v \Rightarrow v = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$

By conservation of mechanical energy, the loss in kinetic energy is stored as the potential energy of the spring:

$\dfrac{1}{2} m_1 u_1^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} (m_1 + m_2) v^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} k x^2$

where $x$ is the maximum compression of the spring.

$\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \times (1.0)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times (0.5)^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times x^2$

$1.0 = 2.0 \times 0.25 + 50 x^2$

$1.0 = 0.5 + 50 x^2$

$50 x^2 = 0.5$

$x^2 = \dfrac{0.5}{50} = \dfrac{1}{100} \text{ m}^2$

$x = 0.1 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: The blocks shown in the figure possess equal masses. The contact surface of block A with the floor is smooth, while that of block B has a friction coefficient of $0.10$ with the floor. Block A travels at a speed of $10 \text{ m/s}$ towards block B, which is initially at rest. Determine the distance travelled by block B if the collision is perfectly elastic. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Given that the blocks have equal masses ($m_A = m_B = m$) and the collision is perfectly elastic, the velocities of the blocks are exchanged after the collision.

Initial velocity of block A, $u_A = 10 \text{ m/s}$
Initial velocity of block B, $u_B = 0$

After the collision, the velocity of block B becomes $v_B = u_A = 10 \text{ m/s}$.

Block B moves on a rough surface with a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.10$.
The retardation of block B is given by $a = \mu g$.
Substituting the given values, $a = 0.10 \times 10 = 1 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Using the equation of motion $v^2 = u^2 - 2as$ for block B coming to rest ($v = 0$):
$0 = (10)^2 - 2(1)s$
$2s = 100$
$s = 50 \text{ m}$

The distance travelled by block B is $50 \text{ m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: As shown in the figure, the coefficient of friction between the horizontal surface and each of the blocks is $0.20$. Given that the collision between the blocks is perfectly elastic, determine the separation between the two blocks when they finally come to rest. Use $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the initial velocity of the $2 \text{ kg}$ block be $u = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$. The retardation due to friction is $a = \mu g = 0.20 \times 10 = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$.

The velocity $v$ of the $2 \text{ kg}$ block just before the collision can be found using the kinematic equation:
$v^2 = u^2 - 2as$
$v^2 = (1.0)^2 - 2(2)(0.16) = 1 - 0.64 = 0.36$
$v = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$

Let $v_1'$ and $v_2'$ be the velocities of the $2 \text{ kg}$ and $4 \text{ kg}$ blocks respectively, just after the perfectly elastic collision. Using the formulas for one-dimensional elastic collision with the second body at rest:
$v_1' = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1 + m_2}v = \dfrac{2 - 4}{2 + 4}(0.6) = -\dfrac{2}{6}(0.6) = -0.2 \text{ m/s}$
$v_2' = \dfrac{2m_1}{m_1 + m_2}v = \dfrac{2(2)}{2 + 4}(0.6) = \dfrac{4}{6}(0.6) = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$

The negative sign indicates that the $2 \text{ kg}$ block rebounds and moves to the left, while the $4 \text{ kg}$ block moves to the right.

Now, both blocks will slide and come to rest due to friction. The retardation for both is $a = \mu g = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Distance traveled by the $2 \text{ kg}$ block before coming to rest:
$s_1 = \dfrac{|v_1'|^2}{2a} = \dfrac{(0.2)^2}{2(2)} = \dfrac{0.04}{4} = 0.01 \text{ m} = 1 \text{ cm}$

Distance traveled by the $4 \text{ kg}$ block before coming to rest:
$s_2 = \dfrac{|v_2'|^2}{2a} = \dfrac{(0.4)^2}{2(2)} = \dfrac{0.16}{4} = 0.04 \text{ m} = 4 \text{ cm}$

Since the blocks move in opposite directions from the point of collision, their final separation is:
$s = s_1 + s_2 = 1 \text{ cm} + 4 \text{ cm} = 5 \text{ cm}$

Answer: $5 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A block of mass $m$ is positioned on a triangular block of mass $M$, which in turn rests on a horizontal surface as shown in the figure. Assuming all surfaces are frictionless, determine the velocity of the triangular block when the smaller block reaches the bottom end.
questions.solution:
Let $V$ be the velocity of the triangular block of mass $M$ towards the left.
Let $v_r$ be the velocity of the block of mass $m$ relative to the triangular block, directed down the incline.
The velocity of the block $m$ relative to the ground is $\vec{v} = \vec{v}_r + \vec{V}$.
Taking the right direction as positive $x$ and upward as positive $y$, the velocity vectors are:
$\vec{V} = -V \hat{i}$
$\vec{v}_r = v_r \cos\alpha \hat{i} - v_r \sin\alpha \hat{j}$
$\vec{v} = (v_r \cos\alpha - V) \hat{i} - v_r \sin\alpha \hat{j}$

Since there is no external force in the horizontal direction, the horizontal momentum of the system is conserved.
Initial horizontal momentum is zero.
$M(-V) + m(v_r \cos\alpha - V) = 0$
$m v_r \cos\alpha = (M + m) V$
$v_r = \dfrac{(M + m) V}{m \cos\alpha}$

By conservation of mechanical energy, the loss in potential energy equals the gain in kinetic energy:
$mgh = \dfrac{1}{2} M V^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$
where $v^2 = (v_r \cos\alpha - V)^2 + (-v_r \sin\alpha)^2 = v_r^2 + V^2 - 2 v_r V \cos\alpha$

Substituting $v_r$ into the expression for $v^2$:
$v^2 = \left( \dfrac{(M + m) V}{m \cos\alpha} \right)^2 + V^2 - 2 \left( \dfrac{(M + m) V}{m \cos\alpha} \right) V \cos\alpha$
$v^2 = V^2 \left[ \dfrac{(M + m)^2}{m^2 \cos^2\alpha} + 1 - \dfrac{2(M + m)}{m} \right]$

Substituting $v^2$ into the energy equation:
$mgh = \dfrac{1}{2} M V^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} m V^2 \left[ \dfrac{(M + m)^2}{m^2 \cos^2\alpha} + 1 - \dfrac{2(M + m)}{m} \right]$
Multiplying by $2$ and expanding:
$2mgh = M V^2 + m V^2 \left[ \dfrac{(M + m)^2}{m^2 \cos^2\alpha} + 1 - \dfrac{2(M + m)}{m} \right]$
$2mgh = V^2 \left[ M + \dfrac{(M + m)^2}{m \cos^2\alpha} + m - 2(M + m) \right]$
$2mgh = V^2 \left[ (M + m) - 2(M + m) + \dfrac{(M + m)^2}{m \cos^2\alpha} \right]$
$2mgh = V^2 \left[ \dfrac{(M + m)^2}{m \cos^2\alpha} - (M + m) \right]$
$2mgh = V^2 (M + m) \left[ \dfrac{M + m}{m \cos^2\alpha} - 1 \right]$
$2mgh = V^2 (M + m) \left[ \dfrac{M + m - m \cos^2\alpha}{m \cos^2\alpha} \right]$
$2mgh = V^2 (M + m) \left[ \dfrac{M + m \sin^2\alpha}{m \cos^2\alpha} \right]$

Solving for $V$:
$V^2 = \dfrac{2m^2 gh \cos^2\alpha}{(M + m)(M + m \sin^2\alpha)}$
$V = \left[ \dfrac{2m^2 gh \cos^2\alpha}{(M + m)(M + m \sin^2\alpha)} \right]^{1/2}$

Answer: $\left[\dfrac{2m^2 gh\cos^2\alpha}{(M + m)(M + m\sin^2\alpha)}\right]^{1/2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a small body of mass $m$ is placed over a larger mass $M$ whose surface is horizontal near the small body and gradually curves to become vertical. The small body is pushed on the larger mass at a speed $v$ and the system is subsequently left to itself. Assume all surfaces are frictionless. Determine the speed of the larger block when the small body is sliding on the vertical part.
questions.solution:
Since all surfaces are frictionless, there is no external horizontal force acting on the system comprising the small body of mass $m$ and the larger mass $M$. Therefore, the horizontal momentum of the system is conserved.

Let the initial velocity of the small mass be $v$. The initial horizontal momentum of the system is:
$P_i = mv$

When the small body reaches the vertical part of the larger mass, it can only move vertically relative to the larger mass. This means its horizontal velocity must be equal to the horizontal velocity of the larger mass. Let this common horizontal velocity be $V$.

The final horizontal momentum of the system at this instant is:
$P_f = (M + m)V$

By the principle of conservation of linear momentum in the horizontal direction:
$P_i = P_f$
$\Rightarrow mv = (M + m)V$

Solving for the speed of the larger block $V$, we get:
$V = \dfrac{mv}{M + m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A small block of superdense material has a mass of $3 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$. It is situated at a height $h$ (much smaller than the earth's radius) from where it falls towards the earth's surface. Determine its speed when its height from the earth's surface has decreased to $h/2$. The mass of the earth is $6 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the block be $m = 3 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$ and the mass of the earth be $M = 6 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}$. We have $M = 2m$.

Since no external forces act on the earth-block system, the center of mass remains at rest. By conservation of linear momentum:
$M v_1 = m v_2$
$2m v_1 = m v_2 \Rightarrow v_1 = \dfrac{v_2}{2}$
where $v_1$ and $v_2$ are the speeds of the earth and the block, respectively.

The height of the block from the earth's surface decreases from $h$ to $h/2$, meaning the relative distance between them decreases by $h/2$. Since $h$ is much smaller than the earth's radius $R$, the gravitational force remains approximately constant:
$F = \dfrac{GMm}{R^2} = mg$

The loss in gravitational potential energy of the system is:
$\Delta U = F \times \Delta r = mg \left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)$

This loss in potential energy is converted into the kinetic energy of both the earth and the block. By conservation of energy:
$\Delta K = \Delta U$
$\dfrac{1}{2} M v_1^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2 = mg \dfrac{h}{2}$

Substituting $M = 2m$ and $v_1 = \dfrac{v_2}{2}$:
$\dfrac{1}{2} (2m) \left(\dfrac{v_2}{2}\right)^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2 = mg \dfrac{h}{2}$

$\dfrac{1}{4} m v_2^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} m v_2^2 = mg \dfrac{h}{2}$

$\dfrac{3}{4} m v_2^2 = mg \dfrac{h}{2}$

$v_2^2 = \dfrac{2gh}{3}$

$v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2gh}{3}}$

Answer: $\sqrt{\dfrac{2gh}{3}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: A small particle travelling with a velocity $v$ undergoes an elastic collision with a spherical body of equal mass and radius $r$ initially kept at rest. As shown in the figure, the centre of the spherical body is situated a distance $\rho\,(< r)$ away from the particle's direction of motion. Determine the final velocities of the two particles. [Hint: The force acts along the normal to the sphere through the contact. Treat the collision as one-dimensional for this direction. In the tangential direction no force acts and the velocities do not change].
options: Small particle moves along the initial direction with speed $v\dfrac{\rho}{r}$; spherical body moves perpendicular to the initial direction with speed $\dfrac{v}{r}\sqrt{r^2 - \rho^2}$
Small particle moves along the tangent with speed $v\dfrac{\rho}{2r}$; spherical body moves perpendicular to the small particle with speed $\dfrac{v}{2r}\sqrt{r^2 - \rho^2}$
Small particle moves along the normal with speed $\dfrac{v}{r}\sqrt{r^2 - \rho^2}$; spherical body moves perpendicular to the small particle with speed $v\dfrac{\rho}{r}$
Small particle moves along the tangent with speed $v\dfrac{\rho}{r}$; spherical body moves perpendicular to the small particle with speed $\dfrac{v}{r}\sqrt{r^2 - \rho^2}$
questions.solution:
Let the angle between the initial velocity of the small particle and the line of impact (normal to the sphere at the point of contact) be $\theta$.

From the geometry of the collision, $\sin\theta = \dfrac{\rho}{r}$ and $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sqrt{r^2 - \rho^2}}{r}$.

The initial velocity of the small particle can be resolved into two components:
Normal component: $v_n = v \cos\theta$
Tangential component: $v_t = v \sin\theta$

The spherical body is initially at rest. Since the collision is elastic and the masses of the two bodies are equal, they exchange their velocities along the line of impact (normal direction).

Final normal velocity of the small particle $= 0$
Final normal velocity of the spherical body $= v \cos\theta$

The tangential components of the velocities remain unchanged because no force acts in the tangential direction.

Final tangential velocity of the small particle $= v \sin\theta$
Final tangential velocity of the spherical body $= 0$

Therefore, the small particle moves along the tangent with speed $v \sin\theta = v\dfrac{\rho}{r}$.

The spherical body moves along the normal with speed $v \cos\theta = \dfrac{v}{r}\sqrt{r^2 - \rho^2}$.

Since the normal and tangent are perpendicular to each other, the spherical body moves perpendicular to the small particle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: Two blocks having masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are connected by a spring of spring constant $k$ and placed on a smooth horizontal surface. Each block is pulled by a constant force $F$ in opposite directions. Find the maximum elongation of the spring and the distances moved by the blocks of masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ respectively till that instant.
questions.solution:
Let the acceleration of the centre of mass be $a = \dfrac{F - (-F)}{m_1 + m_2} = \dfrac{2F}{m_1 + m_2}$.

In the centre of mass frame, each block experiences an effective force stretching the spring. The relative acceleration between the two blocks is:
$a_{rel} = \dfrac{F}{m_1} + \dfrac{F}{m_2} = F\left(\dfrac{1}{m_1} + \dfrac{1}{m_2}\right) = \dfrac{F(m_1 + m_2)}{m_1 m_2}$

Using work-energy principle for relative motion:
$\dfrac{1}{2} \mu v_{rel}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} k x_{max}^2$, where $\mu = \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$

Also, effective force in relative motion is $2F$, so maximum extension occurs when:
$k x_{max} = 2F \Rightarrow x_{max} = \dfrac{2F}{k}$

Distances moved are in inverse ratio of masses:
$x_1 = \dfrac{2F m_2}{k(m_1 + m_2)}, \quad x_2 = \dfrac{2F m_1}{k(m_1 + m_2)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Centre of Mass, Linear momentum, Collision
questions.q: Two blocks possess equal masses. The contact surface of block A with the floor is smooth, while that of block B has a friction coefficient of $0.10$ with the floor. Block A travels at a speed of $10 \text{ m/s}$ towards block B, which is initially at rest. Determine the distance travelled by block B if the collision is perfectly inelastic. Take $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$.
questions.solution:
Let the mass of each block be $m$.

By the principle of conservation of linear momentum during the perfectly inelastic collision, the common velocity $v$ of the blocks after they stick together is given by:
$m u_A + m u_B = (m + m) v$
$m(10) + m(0) = 2mv$
$10m = 2mv \Rightarrow v = 5 \text{ m/s}$

After the collision, the blocks move together as a single system of mass $2m$.
The frictional force acts only on block B, as the surface under block A is smooth.
Frictional force, $f = \mu N_B = \mu mg = 0.10 \times m \times 10 = m$

The deceleration $a$ of the combined system is:
$a = \dfrac{f}{\text{Total mass}} = \dfrac{m}{2m} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$

Using the third equation of motion to find the distance $s$ travelled before coming to rest ($v_f = 0$):
$v_f^2 = v^2 - 2as$
$0 = (5)^2 - 2(0.5)s$
$25 = 1 \cdot s \Rightarrow s = 25 \text{ m}$
